channel.title: Responsible AI & Neurodivergence, Naba Rizvi
channel.description: Daily curated research feed on responsible AI, neurodivergence, and serious mental illness.

item.title: What to Make of AI Psychosis, Harvard Gazette, 2026
item.description: John Torous explains why the wave of AI psychosis patients he expected never arrived, and why he and coauthors Matthew Flathers and Spencer Roux propose sorting cases by the role the model plays: catalyst of new symptoms, amplifier of existing ones, coauthor of an escalating narrative, or object of a delusional belief. He argues that AI acting as a true catalyst is very rare, that amplifier and object roles are more common, and that lumping everything under one label drowns out the cases that are real. Named risk factors are conversations running to thousands of messages, ascribing sentience to the chatbot, and voice rather than text interaction.

item.title: Automated Detection and Classification of Delusion related Content in Naturalistic Audio Diaries Using Multi Agent Language Models, CLPsych 2026
item.description: A University of Washington team builds a multi agent LLM pipeline for fine grained, multi label extraction of delusional beliefs and the affective and behavioral responses attached to them, working from transcripts of audio diaries kept by people with moderate persecutory ideation. Across an ensemble of three foundation models, detailed diagnostic prompt instructions cut false positives on delusional theme classification but also narrowed how the models read affective and behavioral responses.

item.title: Intra Fairness Dynamics: The Bias Spillover Effect in Targeted LLM Alignment, arXiv, 2026
item.description: Researchers at DTU and the University of Copenhagen align Mistral 7B, Llama 3.1 8B, and Qwen 2.5 7B for gender fairness using Direct Preference Optimization, then re measure fairness across nine sensitive attributes on the BBQ benchmark. Aggregate numbers improve, but context aware analysis shows significant degradation in ambiguous contexts for physical appearance, sexual orientation, and disability status, meaning a targeted debias can push harm onto untargeted groups.

item.title: Application of Artificial Intelligence and Psychosocial Functioning in Psychosis: A Systematic Review and Meta Analysis, Frontiers in Psychiatry, 2025
item.description: Fourteen studies covering social function, occupational function, social cognition, and quality of life are pooled under PRISMA, with supervised machine learning the dominant technique. Performance is moderate rather than clinically decisive, a pooled AUC of 0.70 and RMSE of 8.15, with better prediction for social cognition, and the authors flag methodological inconsistency, data quality, and ethical issues as reasons the field remains early.

item.title: Framing Responsible Design of AI for Mental Well Being: AI as Primary Care, Nutritional Supplement, or Yoga Instructor?, arXiv, 2026
item.description: Cornell, Washington, Weill Cornell, and USC researchers interview experts and read the relevant regulation to ask what responsible design means for the nonclinical LLM tools millions already use for mental well being. They land on two questions a developer must answer: what specific benefit the tool guarantees and for whom, and whether it guarantees effective delivery of its active ingredients, using the supplement, drug, yoga instructor, and primary care analogies to fix risks, evaluation metrics, and who is accountable.

item.title: Alignment Plausibility: A New Standard for Assuring AI in Healthcare, arXiv, 2026
item.description: Gwydion Williams, Sara Zannone, and Bilal A Mateen take up the regulator's problem: deciding whether a generative system is aligned to good outcomes when its behavior is nondeterministic and cannot be exhaustively tested. They propose alignment plausibility as an assurance standard for judging safety and effectiveness under that uncertainty.
